name: Wandjina
description: The Wandjina are cloud and rain spirits from Aboriginal mythology, particularly significant to the Worrorra, Ngarinyin, and Wunambal peoples of the Kimberley region in Western Australia. They are powerful creators and controllers of the weather, bringing life-giving rain during the wet season. The Wandjina are typically depicted in rock art as figures with large eyes, no mouths, and headdresses or halos. Their lack of mouths is often interpreted as a symbol of their immense power, as they communicate through telepathy or the power of creation itself. They are associated with water sources, such as rivers, springs, and rock pools, and are believed to reside in these places during the dry season. The Wandjina are deeply respected and feared, and their images are treated with great reverence. Maintaining their paintings is a sacred duty, ensuring the continuation of the life cycle and the abundance of rain. Disrupting or defacing Wandjina art can bring about catastrophic weather events, demonstrating their ongoing influence on the natural world and the lives of the people who depend on it. These beings are ancestral figures, representing the power of creation and the vital connection between the people and the land. They are guardians of the law and maintainers of the balance of nature. They are involved in ceremonies and continue to be important figures in the cultural and spiritual life of the Aboriginal people in the Kimberley region.
subtitle: Cloud and rain spirits of Kimberley
appearance: Wandjina are typically depicted with large, round eyes, no mouths, and a headdress or halo around their heads. Their bodies are often depicted as pale or white, sometimes with markings or patterns. They are often shown with multiple arms or hands, emphasizing their power and ability to influence the world around them.
habitat: Wandjina reside in the Kimberley region of Western Australia, particularly in and around water sources such as rivers, springs, rock pools, and caves. They are closely associated with the wet season and the life-giving rains it brings.
behavior: Wandjina are powerful and respected figures, revered for their role in creating and maintaining the natural world. They are believed to be active during the wet season, bringing rain and ensuring the fertility of the land. They are also seen as guardians of the law, punishing those who disrupt the balance of nature or disrespect their images.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Kimberley
keyMyths.description: The Wandjina created the landscape of the Kimberley, shaping the mountains, rivers, and valleys. They also created the first people and taught them how to live in harmony with the land.
keyMyths.source: Oral traditions of the Worrorra, Ngarinyin, and Wunambal peoples

keyMyths.title: The First Rains
keyMyths.description: The Wandjina are responsible for bringing the first rains of the wet season, ensuring the survival of all living things. Their images are painted to ensure the continuation of this vital cycle.

sources.author: Grahame Walsh
sources.work: Bradshaws: Ancient Rock Paintings of North-West Australia
sources.description: Provides detailed information on rock art in the Kimberley region, including depictions of Wandjina.
sources.text: Provides detailed information on rock art in the Kimberley region, including depictions of Wandjina.

sources.author: Howard Morphy
sources.work: Ancestral Connections: Art and an Aboriginal System of Knowledge
sources.description: Explores the relationship between Aboriginal art and knowledge systems, including the role of the Wandjina.
sources.text: Explores the relationship between Aboriginal art and knowledge systems, including the role of the Wandjina.
